concepts_tested: puberty as cultural signal of adult roles
legal and ceremonial markers vs biological maturity
impact of socioeconomic status on pathways to adulthood

questions.question: How do hidden transnational financial networks let corruption cross borders?

options.A: They layer and route illicit funds through many countries and shell entities, hiding the true owners and making audits and prosecutions much harder.
options.B: They force all money to pass through a single, fully auditable country, which makes detection easy.
options.C: They use simple, transparent transactions that leave no room for doubt, lowering risk for corrupt actors.
options.D: They only affect domestic corruption and have no role in cross-border corruption.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, removed dense phrasing, explained 'layer and route' plainly, kept the original core idea about hiding ownership and hindering enforcement.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Corruption as the abuse of entrusted power for private gain, encompassing a spectrum from illegal acts to legally ambiguous practices like lobbying.
Concept 2: Mechanisms and enabling structures of corruption, including illegal acts (bribery, embezzlement) and hidden/transnational financial networks that facilitate corruption.
Concept 3: The relationship between corruption and governance/anti-corruption efforts, including policy responses, international initiatives, and debates about measurement and perception (e.g., CPI).

questions.question: According to elite theory, why do a small group's leaders keep power even when democratic elections take place?

options.A: Elections guarantee regular turnover, so no elite stays dominant.
options.B: Elite networks reinforce their power across institutions, making their influence long-lasting and pluralism seem unattainable.
options.C: Ordinary people use informal channels to match elites and balance power equally.
options.D: Merit-based selection makes influence follow the public interest and removes inequality.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question, used clear terms like 'small group's leaders' and 'elite networks'; kept the idea that elites maintain power despite elections and that pluralism is viewed as unrealistic by elite theory.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Elites and legitimacy of influence. The idea that a select group with desirable qualities is more likely to contribute constructively and deserve greater influence, shaping governance and social order.

Concept 2: Competing frameworks for power distribution. The contrast between elite theory (which sees elite influence as real and pluralism as utopian) and other views like pluralism or egalitarianism, highlighting mechanisms of political power.

Concept 3: Criteria for elite status and its relation to social structures. How elite status arises (achievement, lineage, wealth) and its connection to social stratification (upper/middle/lower classes) and to governance systems (technocracy, meritocracy, plutocracy).

questions.question: Cosmopolitanism holds that people have universal moral duties but cultures have different norms. Which approach best lets universal duties coexist with cultural diversity to build global solidarity?

options.A: Enforce the same laws everywhere so everyone follows identical moral standards.
options.B: Use context-sensitive moral reasoning: interpret universal duties in ways that respect local norms while keeping core ethical commitments, enabling cross-cultural cooperation without cultural imperialism.
options.C: Let economic markets and integration drive behaviour, assuming market forces will align actions with universal duties.
options.D: Replace national sovereignty with a single global authority that imposes universal duties on all cultures.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to two sentences, used simpler wording, and made each option concise while keeping the original choices and meaning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Cosmopolitanism as a universal human community with mutual respect across diverse beliefs and locations.
Concept 2: The multiplicity of forms (moral, political, cultural, economic) and how they interact or conflict in realizing cosmopolitan aims.
Concept 3: The role of global political structures and universal standards as mechanisms to privilege global sociality and coordinated engagement among all humans.

questions.question: The triad that shapes user experience is the system, the user, and the context of use. Why might a software update that speeds up tasks not improve UX for every user in every situation?

options.A: Because UX is determined only by the system; if the system is faster, all users in all contexts should have better UX.
options.B: Because a user’s feelings, goals, past experience, and the context (environment, interruptions, other tasks) change how they value speed; the same efficiency gain may help some users but not others or even harm their experience.
options.C: Because once a task is completed faster, context cannot affect perceived usability; only completion time matters.
options.D: Because faster systems always lower cognitive load for everyone, so they guarantee a better UX for all users.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, kept the triad (system, user, context), and used simple examples (feelings, goals, interruptions) to explain why speed may not always improve UX.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The relationship between usability and user experience (pragmatic vs. hedonic aspects) and how improvements in usability can influence overall UX.
Concept 2: The triad of factors shaping UX — the system, the user, and the context of use — and how each factor can alter the user experience.

Concept 3: The subjective nature of UX versus objective attributes, including how emotions/beliefs influence UX and how business objectives (e.g., profitability) can conflict with ethical UX considerations.

questions.question: Why can objects carry messages and connect people across time and distance?

options.A: Because objects have fixed, unchanging meanings that pass messages on their own.
options.B: Because objects accumulate histories, memories, and shared uses, so they can convey meaning across time and place.
options.C: Because objects are only about appearance and do not affect social relationships.
options.D: Because objects force people to follow the owner's intentions, making communication clear and unambiguous.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options, removed technical jargon, and emphasized ‘histories, memories, and shared uses’ to keep the original idea that objects mediate social ties over time and space.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Objects mediate social relationships and communication across time and space; material culture is about the relationships between people and their things.
Concept 2: Meaning and value of objects arise from social/psychological processes (monetary value, sentimental value, endowment effect, memory, identity, social standing).
Concept 3: Gift-giving functions as a vehicle of social obligation and political maneuver within material culture, influencing relationships and social dynamics.

questions.question: Why does the sorites paradox show that some vague predicates resist a simple true/false (bivalent) assignment?

options.A: Because if we assign true/false to each case and assume every small step preserves truth, a chain from a clearly true instance to a clearly false instance yields a contradiction, so no fixed true/false assignment can capture the predicate.
options.B: Because vagueness requires more than two truth values for each statement, so a multi-valued logic is needed.
options.C: Because borderline cases are actually multiple meanings of the predicate (ambiguity), which prevents a single truth value.
options.D: Because legal systems must be precise, so vagueness cannot exist in natural language.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options to plain language; kept the sorites chain/contradiction idea as the main point and preserved contrasts with ambiguity and legal precision.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Borderline cases and the sorites paradox as central to vagueness, illustrating why some predicates resist bivalent truth valuations.
Concept 2: The distinction between vagueness and ambiguity and its implications for linguistic meaning and interpretation.
Concept 3: The role of vagueness in law and governance (e.g., void for vagueness, handling borderline cases in legal standards) and its relation to formal semantic modeling.

questions.question: Which idea best explains how moral judgments become real actions, and why people sometimes act against their own moral judgments?

options.A: Kohlberg’s six-stage theory says moral reasoning develops the same way for everyone and always determines action, so there should be no gap between judgment and behavior.
options.B: Blasi’s self-model says judgments lead to action only when they are part of a person’s self-concept through moral commitment, so gaps happen when that commitment is weak.
options.C: The gap exists because moral judgments sit in a separate cognitive module from action plans, and only immediate emotions reliably trigger behavior.
options.D: Actions are driven only by social pressures and rewards, so internal moral judgments rarely affect behavior unless the social environment reinforces them.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, removed complex wording, kept the three main theories (Kohlberg, Blasi, cognitive/emotional, social pressure) and preserved the correct choice.
concepts_tested: The relationship between moral judgment/reasoning and moral action, including observed gaps and factors that mediate translation from judgment to behavior
Kohlberg’s stages of moral reasoning and the idea of universal development across cultures
Blasi’s self-model as a mechanism linking moral judgment to action via moral commitment

questions.question: Why do law and economics scholars use economic efficiency to judge legal rules?

options.A: Because efficiency guarantees that every person will be made better off.
options.B: Because it aims to maximize total net social benefits by aligning incentives and reducing wasted resources and transaction costs, which raises overall social welfare.
options.C: Because efficiency is mainly about making legal procedures simpler and faster to enforce.
options.D: Because efficiency requires equalizing outcomes for all individuals.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence and made each option concise; kept the core idea that efficiency is used to maximize net social benefits by aligning incentives and reducing costs.
concepts_tested: Economic efficiency as a criterion for evaluating legal rules
The two major analytical branches: neoclassical analysis of law and institutional analysis of legal institutions
Application of microeconomic concepts (incentives, costs/benefits, effects) to analyze and predict legal outcomes

questions.question: Why do law and economics scholars use efficiency to judge legal rules and predict which rules will be made?

options.A: To try to raise total social welfare by lowering transaction costs and aligning incentives, while noting enforcement and information limits.
options.B: Because efficiency always ensures fair distribution of wealth, so efficient rules are always chosen.
options.C: Because lawmakers mainly follow public opinion, and efficiency is just a cover.
options.D: Because only rules that achieve perfect competition last, and efficiency guarantees that outcome.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and simplified wording, removed technical jargon, kept the core idea that efficiency links to social welfare, transaction costs, incentives, and enforcement/information limits.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Economic efficiency as a criterion for evaluating legal rules and predicting which rules will be promulgated
Concept 2: Two main analytical branches—neoclassical analysis of law (positive and normative) and institutional analysis focusing on law and governance
Concept 3: Application of microeconomic theory to explain the effects of laws and to analyze the impact of legal rules on outcomes

questions.question: A city compares two projects using CBA with discount rate r = 0.10: the Park costs \$2,000 now and gives benefits \$1,000 at the end of years 1–3; the Road costs \$2,500 now and gives benefits \$2,000 in year 1 and \$500 in years 2–3. Using $\mathrm{NPV}=\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\frac{B_t-C_t}{(1+r)^t}$, which statement is correct?

options.A: Choose the Park: its NPV ≈ \$487 vs the Road's ≈ \$107, so CBA ranks the Park higher; however, uncertainty in benefit estimates or the discount rate could change the preferred project.
options.B: Choose the Road because it gives the largest benefit in year 1 and CBA always prefers projects with bigger early-year benefits regardless of NPV.
options.C: No decision can be made because CBA cannot compare projects that have different time patterns of benefits.
options.D: Choose the Park only if the cost–benefit ratio exceeds 2:1; since both projects have ratios below 2:1, neither should be built.

questions.generation_notes: Constructed a concrete 3-year example, computed present values to compare NPVs and created distractors testing misunderstandings about discounting, NPV decision rule, and limits due to uncertainty.
concepts_tested: Monetization and time value of money (NPV/discounting)
Decision rule and welfare comparison (choose higher NPV/cost–benefit ranking)
Limitations and uncertainty of estimates affecting CBA outcomes

questions.question: Why do we make future money worth less when adding costs and benefits today?

options.A: A discount rate shows money today is worth more than money later.
options.B: A discount rate only adjusts for price changes.
options.C: It makes all future money count the same.
options.D: It turns costs negative and benefits positive.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words. Kept the idea that money now is worth more.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Monetary valuation and time value of money (net present value) as the basis for comparing benefits and costs across time.
Concept 2: Using a cost–benefit ratio to rank options and determine which action increases welfare (and its relation to Pareto efficiency).
Concept 3: Limitations and uncertainties in CBA (accuracy of estimates, potential manipulation by interest groups) and their impact on decisions.

questions.question: A British town in 1780 moves textile production from cottages to a water‑powered mill using machine tools; entrepreneurs patent the machines and banks finance the factory; within decades similar mills appear in the US and Belgium. Which three explanations best account for this change and its spread?

options.A: Mechanization (water/steam power and machine tools) replacing hand production; legal protections (patents, property rights) and access to capital plus entrepreneurial initiative; and Britain's textile demand, ports and colonial cotton supply that let mills scale and diffuse abroad.
options.B: Strict state control and high tariffs that forced factory creation; abundant slave or coerced labour supplying very cheap workers; and a cultural preference for machine‑made goods over handcrafts.
options.C: Rapid urban population growth and famine pushing people into cities; guilds blocking machine use so factories hired only unskilled children; and local religious movements promoting factory work.
options.D: Improved agriculture that freed workers for factories; the introduction of steam and water power with new machine tools raising output; and courts enforcing property rights while banks lent to industrialists.

questions.generation_notes: Created a concise scenario showing mechanization, institutions, and geographic/sectoral diffusion; options mix plausible but incorrect causes to test students' ability to link technology, legal/financial institutions, and Britain's textile/trade advantages.
concepts_tested: mechanization and energy shift (water/steam, machine tools, factory system)
institutional framework (property rights, patents, access to capital, entrepreneurial culture)
diffusion and sectoral/geographic spread (textiles as lead industry, Britain’s advantage, spread to Europe/US)

questions.question: In a public–private partnership (PPP), why must contracts carefully assign risk and align incentives to get value for money, and what trade-offs can that create?

options.A: It guarantees the private partner gets higher fixed payments no matter the results, removing risk from the public sector.
options.B: It makes the private partner bear design, construction and performance risks so they are motivated to lower lifecycle costs and keep quality, but excessive risk transfer or misaligned incentives can cause inflated bids or risk-averse behaviour that reduces efficiency.
options.C: It ensures all financial risk is placed on taxpayers, making the private partner effectively risk-free.
options.D: It has no effect because payments are fixed and independent of how well the project performs.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, removed dense jargon, kept key terms (risk transfer, incentives, lifecycle costs) and explained the trade-off succinctly; answer choices were made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Risk transfer and incentive alignment in PPP contracts
Concept 2: Financing structure and lifecycle costs (private funding up-front funded by taxes/users, and implications for public budgets)
Concept 3: Accountability, transparency, and evaluation challenges in PPPs (secrecy of financial details, mixed evidence on value for money)

questions.question: How does adaptive management help make ecosystems more resilient and sustainable over the long term?

options.A: It sets a fixed, unchanging plan that cannot be altered by new ecological or social information.
options.B: It uses repeated cycles of action, monitoring, evaluation, and adjustment, treating actions as experiments to learn and improve management.
options.C: It concentrates only on ecological measures and ignores social or political factors to keep ecosystems untouched.
options.D: It aims to boost short-term economic gains without using feedback from the ecosystem to change decisions.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; kept key term 'adaptive management'; answer choices made concise and clear while preserving original meanings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Holistic integration of ecological health with human social, political, and cultural needs as a governing principle.
Concept 2: Resilience and long-term sustainability as guiding goals, with adaptive management as the mechanism to achieve them.
Concept 3: Context-dependent implementation and diverse governance forms (adaptive management, strategic management, landscape-scale conservation) driven by stakeholder participation.

questions.question: A coastal town has fewer fish and more floods. Which plan best follows ecosystem management principles?

options.A: Bring fishers, scientists, and officials together to restore wetlands, monitor outcomes, and change rules as needed.
options.B: Ban fishing and build high seawalls without local input to stop floods and protect fish.
options.C: Turn wetlands into shrimp farms to boost short-term income and increase fish harvests.
options.D: Let nature decide with no monitoring or plans and hope fish populations recover.

questions.generation_notes: Wrote a short, concrete coastal scenario and gave four realistic plans; the correct option shows stakeholder collaboration, landscape restoration, monitoring, and adaptive change.
concepts_tested: Holistic, adaptive management integrating ecological and social knowledge
How ecosystem management improves resilience versus command-and-control
Stakeholder participation and landscape-scale restoration to balance social and ecological goals

questions.question: A city models traffic as a system of roads, signals, and commuter behavior; engineers use a black-box controller that adjusts green-light duration based on measured speed and linearize the controller near the desired average speed to test stability. Which statement best demonstrates systems thinking applied to this scenario?

options.A: Treats traffic as an interconnected whole, models the controller as a black box with feedback, linearizes near the equilibrium to check stability, and looks for leverage points such as small timing changes that yield large improvements.
options.B: Focuses on improving a single road by widening lanes, assuming other parts of the system won't affect traffic flow.
options.C: Relies on the linearized model to predict behavior for very large deviations from the target speed and plans major changes from that model.
options.D: Uses the feedback controller but ignores system structure and tries many random adjustments until congestion decreases.

questions.generation_notes: Created a compact applied scenario (city traffic) that requires recognition of system wholes, feedback/linearization for stability, black-box modeling, and finding leverage points; distractors show common misunderstandings.
concepts_tested: Systems as wholes and interrelationships
Feedback control and linearization for dynamical stability
Black-box modeling and leverage points for system change

questions.question: A coastal city wants to reduce plastic pollution. Which plan best follows environmental governance, treating the economy as part of the environment and shifting to closed-loop systems?

options.A: Local, national, and international actors plus businesses and community groups set rules, redesign products, and run city-wide recycling loops.
options.B: City government fines plastic users and hires cleanup crews, but keeps existing production and disposal methods.
options.C: A private company pays for beach cleanups and ships collected trash to other countries for disposal.
options.D: An international treaty sets targets but gives no help to cities or businesses to change practices.

questions.generation_notes: Created a coastal pollution scenario requiring choice that combines multi-level actor collaboration, embedding environment in decisions, and closed-loop solutions.
concepts_tested: Multi-level, multi-actor governance (local, national, international; state, market, civil society)
Embedding the environment in decision-making and viewing economy as part of the environment
Transition from cradle-to-grave to closed-loop/cradle-to-cradle systems (recycling, product redesign)

questions.question: Why does help from local, national, and global groups make nature rules work better?

options.A: It gives one group all power to act faster.
options.B: Sharing knowledge, money, tests, and accountability across levels helps rules work.
options.C: One government can force the same rules in every place.
options.D: It removes the need for talking between levels.

questions.simplification_notes: This version uses simple words about local, national, and global help.
concepts_tested: Multi-level, multi-actor governance: interactions among state, market, and civil society across local, national, and global levels in formulating and implementing environmental policies.
Embedding the environment in decision-making: ensuring environmental considerations permeate all levels of policy and action, and recognizing the connection between people and ecosystems.
Transition to circular/sustainable systems: moving from cradle-to-grave waste models to cradle-to-cradle/closed-loop approaches within governance to promote sustainable development.

questions.question: A town uses a river for farming and fishing, but a new factory pollutes the water. Which plan best shows integrated stewardship and adaptive management?

options.A: Ban the factory immediately by law and never review the decision.
options.B: Form a team of farmers, fishers, scientists and officials to reduce pollution, monitor water, and change actions as needed.
options.C: Sell the river rights to a private company to run for profit.
options.D: Ignore the pollution and hope the fish return on their own.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short, concrete river-pollution scenario asking students to pick the plan that combines social and ecological actors, long-term stewardship, and monitoring with flexible changes (adaptive management).
concepts_tested: Integrated social-ecological approach
Stewardship and life-support capacity
Adaptive governance and management (monitoring and changing actions)

questions.question: An Indigenous village owns the wetland. Which choice shows adaptive rules, village ownership, long-term jobs?

options.A: Village owners count fish monthly to protect jobs.
options.B: A company buys the wetland for profit.
options.C: The government closes the wetland forever.
options.D: Villagers take many fish without rules.

questions.generation_notes: Used a wetland story to test ownership, counting, rule changes, long-term jobs.
concepts_tested: ownership rules
adaptive learning by counting
linking nature to people's jobs

questions.question: Why does religious studies use methods from many disciplines, and how does that change which explanations are seen as credible?

options.A: It requires every claim be confirmed by every discipline, forcing universal agreement across fields.
options.B: It lets scholars triangulate different kinds of evidence and perspectives, so credible explanations can draw on belief, practice, social context, and history rather than one method.
options.C: It aims to defend a single religious worldview by fitting all methods into that viewpoint.
options.D: It sets a strict hierarchy where empirical data always override interpretive insights, narrowing what counts as credible.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; jargon reduced; kept core idea that multiple disciplines allow cross-checking evidence and broaden acceptable explanations. Options made concise and plausible; correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Methodological stance and objectivity. Religious studies emphasizes empirical, historical, and cross-cultural analysis and seeks independence from any single religious viewpoint, often contrasting with theology.

Concept 2: Pluralism of approaches and methods. The field is characterized by using multiple disciplines (anthropology, sociology, psychology, philosophy, history) and being open to many approaches, with no single distinctive method.

Concept 3: Diverse scholarly aims and interpretive stances. Scholars may defend religion, explain it, or use it to test theories, and even study their own religion; these aims influence interpretation and demonstrate relational dynamics within the field.

questions.question: A class studies a holiday using interviews, history books, surveys, and brain scans, without promoting any faith. What best describes this approach?

options.A: Religious studies: uses many disciplines and stays objective.
options.B: Theology: defends a faith and uses scripture.
options.C: Anthropology only: studies culture and rituals.
options.D: Philosophy only: argues about ideas.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short classroom scenario showing use of multiple disciplines and neutral stance; options contrast religious studies with theology and single-discipline views.
concepts_tested: interdisciplinarity and methodological pluralism
religious studies versus theology (objective, non-confessional)
training and methods of inquiry to make religion intelligible

questions.question: Why does letting people voice their concerns during decision-making make the process feel fairer and more legitimate even if the outcome stays the same?

options.A: It changes the chances of a better outcome for the speaker, making the result more equitable.
options.B: It signals respect and value, improving interpersonal treatment and transparency, which shapes how fair the process seems.
options.C: It removes all bias from the final decision by giving everyone a chance to speak.
options.D: It shortens decision time, so the process appears more efficient and therefore more legitimate.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, replaced technical terms with plain language, and made each option concise while keeping the original distinctions between interpersonal effects and outcome changes.
concepts_tested: The impact of giving individuals a voice on perceptions of fairness and legitimacy of decisions
The distinction between procedural justice and distributive justice, including scenarios where fair procedures yield acceptance even with unequal outcomes
The role of interpersonal treatment (respect/value) in procedural justice and its link to employee satisfaction and performance

questions.question: A beetle species lives on three islands. (1) On island A, birds eat brown beetles so the brown allele becomes rarer. (2) Island C is very small and loses the green allele by chance. (3) On island B a single DNA change produced a new brown allele. (4) DNA comparisons show islands A and B share many recent mutations, so they are more closely related than C. Which processes best explain observations (1)–(4) respectively?

options.A: Natural selection; genetic drift; mutation; descent with modification (common ancestry).
options.B: Mutation; genetic drift; gene flow; natural selection.
options.C: Genetic drift; natural selection; mutation; gene flow.
options.D: Gene flow; mutation; genetic drift; descent with modification (common ancestry).

questions.generation_notes: Created a short island beetle scenario mapping each numbered observation to the primary evolutionary process: selection, drift, mutation, and common ancestry/phylogeny.
concepts_tested: Natural selection (differential fitness)
Genetic drift, mutation, gene flow as population-genetic mechanisms
Descent with modification and phylogenetic inference (common ancestry)

questions.question: On an island, most moths were light-coloured until tree bark darkened from pollution and, over several generations, dark moths became common; later a storm brought many light moths from the mainland and a small isolated colony nearby showed random shifts in allele frequencies. Which combination of evolutionary forces best explains these observations?

options.A: Directional natural selection favored dark moths when trees darkened; gene flow from the mainland later reintroduced light alleles; genetic drift (founder effect) caused random changes in the small colony.
options.B: New beneficial mutations produced the dark colour, stabilising selection then maintained both colours, and mutation bias later made light alleles common.
options.C: Sexual selection preferred dark moths initially, horizontal gene transfer brought light-colour genes from the mainland, and natural selection caused random shifts in the small colony.
options.D: Adaptive radiation produced dark moths, speciation then created light and dark species, and directional selection only affected the mainland population.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short island moth scenario to require students to identify directional natural selection, gene flow, and genetic drift (founder effect) as the causes of observed allele-frequency changes.
concepts_tested: natural selection (directional)
gene flow and genetic drift (founder effect)
heritable variation enabling evolution (alleles, inheritance)

questions.question: On Island A, lizards with bigger jaws eat large insects and leave more young. Which explanation best fits why jaws changed and why the lizards are related?

options.A: Heritable jaw-size variation allowed natural selection to favour big jaws. Mutation, drift, and gene flow changed gene frequencies and common ancestry explains similarity.
options.B: Random genetic drift alone made big jaws common. Ancestors are not involved.
options.C: The environment directly changed lizard bones so the trait was not heritable.
options.D: A different species moved in and replaced the lizards, so no evolution occurred.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete island lizard scenario to link heritable variation and natural selection with population processes (mutation, drift, gene flow) and common ancestry; created plausible distractors.
concepts_tested: Natural selection as differential survival and reproduction due to heritable variation
Role of genetic variation and population processes (mutation, genetic drift, gene flow) in changing allele frequencies
Common ancestry and phylogenetic relationships explaining similarity among populations

questions.question: On Island A birds ate most green beetles. Which forces can explain the beetle colour change and the island similarity?

options.A: Natural selection, mutation, genetic drift, gene flow, and shared ancestry caused the patterns.
options.B: Only birds eating green beetles caused the colour change.
options.C: A storm and random chance alone explain both changes.
options.D: New beetles arriving from Island B fixed the brown colour.

questions.generation_notes: Made a beetle island scenario to test selection, drift, mutation, gene flow, and descent.
concepts_tested: Natural selection acting on heritable variation
Other forces changing allele frequencies (mutation, genetic drift, gene flow)
Common descent explaining similarity between populations

questions.question: In the policy cycle, how does the evaluation stage help turn a problem and its policy into a more effective policy in the next cycle?

options.A: It confirms the original problem framing and keeps the same formulation for reuse.
options.B: It compares real outcomes to the policy's objectives, shows which assumptions held or failed, and guides revisions or a restart of the cycle.
options.C: It delegates implementation details to outside actors to try to secure consistent results.
options.D: It secures immediate political support so the policy's legitimacy is permanently fixed.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; retained key policy-cycle terms. Options made concise and focused on outcomes, assumptions, revision, implementation, and legitimacy.
concepts_tested: The policy cycle as a framework for turning problems into policies and evaluating outcomes (stages: agenda setting, formulation, legitimation, implementation, evaluation; includes feedback to inform next steps)
The policy-process actor-network (roles and interactions of elected officials, civil servants, interest groups, experts, and citizens; idea of co-production and legitimacy)
Policy instruments and design (use of regulations, subsidies, taxes, and spending) and how instrument choices influence achieving objectives like growth, equality, or environmental protection

questions.question: In the policy cycle, evaluation checks if a policy worked. How does this result usually affect the next stage?

options.A: It makes the policy stop at once, without checking other options.
options.B: It forces one policy tool to be kept no matter the results or feedback.
options.C: It shows what worked and what failed. Policymakers then adjust goals, redesign tools, or send it back to agenda-setting or formulation.
options.D: It guarantees permanent legitimacy and ends the whole cycle.

questions.simplification_notes: Language shortened and split into two short sentences; removed academic jargon; kept core idea that evaluation gives evidence and can send policy back to earlier stages for revision.
concepts_tested: The policy cycle as a framework linking agenda setting, formulation, legitimation, implementation, and evaluation.
The role of multiple actors and stakeholders in policy formation, including co-production with communities and experts.
Policy instruments and aims: how regulations, subsidies, taxes, and spending are used to achieve social/economic objectives.

questions.question: A city wants to cut air pollution. Which option lists the five policy-cycle steps in the correct order?

options.A: Measure pollution data; citizens and planners co-design bus lanes and subsidies; council passes the plan; transport runs new buses and gives subsidies; officials check results
options.B: Council passes the plan; measure pollution data; transport runs new buses and gives subsidies; citizens co-design bus lanes; officials check results
options.C: Citizens co-design bus lanes and subsidies; transport runs new buses and gives subsidies; measure pollution data; council passes the plan; officials check results
options.D: Measure pollution data; council passes the plan; transport runs new buses and gives subsidies; citizens co-design bus lanes; officials check results

questions.generation_notes: Created a concrete city pollution scenario mapping five actions to the policy-cycle stages (agenda setting, formulation with co-production, legitimation, implementation with instruments, evaluation). Distractors scramble order to test multi-step reasoning.
concepts_tested: policy cycle stages (agenda setting, formulation, legitimation, implementation, evaluation)
policy actors and co-production; policy instruments (subsidies, spending)

questions.question: How does checking a policy's results help make better rules next time?

options.A: It makes leaders repeat the same rule to keep votes.
options.B: It shows if results met goals, pointing to needed changes.
options.C: It blames others for results to avoid fixing the rule.
options.D: It freezes the original problem idea, blocking new aims.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified wording for children, keeping idea of using evaluation to guide changes.
concepts_tested: The policy cycle as a framework (agenda setting, formulation, legitimation, implementation, evaluation) and the role of feedback in shaping future policy.
The network of actors and co-production (politicians, civil servants, interest groups, experts, citizens) and how power dynamics and collaboration influence policy design and outcomes.

The link between policy aims and instruments (how regulations, subsidies, taxes, and spending plans are selected and mapped to objectives like economic growth, inequality reduction, or environmental protection).

questions.question: How does cultural hegemony keep a ruling group's power stable without needing constant force?

options.A: By making the ruling group's worldview seem natural and beneficial, so people consent and obey, letting power persist even if formal rules change.
options.B: By keeping a constant police or military presence to punish dissent and keep people afraid.
options.C: By using treaties and installed governments to extract resources and control subordinate states from outside.
options.D: By randomly selecting leaders and relying on chance rather than any planned strategy.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence and wording simplified; answer choices made concise. Kept core ideas: consent via norms/worldview, contrast with coercion, and external domination.
concepts_tested: Cultural Hegemony as a mechanism of power: the ruling class uses consent and shaping of social norms/worldview to maintain the status quo.
Distinction between hegemony and empire: hegemony involves external predominance with limited or no internal control, differing from direct imperial rule.
Hegemonic order’s effect on subordinate states: it dictates internal politics or installs governments, shaping societal character and political arrangements.

questions.question: A government gives a consumer subsidy for a good with positive external benefits. How does this change resource use and who gains, and when can the subsidy make overall welfare worse?

options.A: It raises the price consumers pay, reduces demand, causes overproduction, and leaves income distribution unchanged.
options.B: It lowers the price consumers pay and raises quantity toward the social optimum. If paid by distortionary taxes, it shifts resources from taxpayers to beneficiaries and can lower welfare when costs exceed benefits.
options.C: It raises firms' costs (shifts supply up) and always improves welfare no matter who pays.
options.D: It removes the externality and guarantees a perfectly efficient outcome with no distribution effects.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex question into two short sentences; used simpler wording and kept key ideas about price, quantity, income effects, and the risk from distortionary taxes.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Justification for government intervention (market failure and redistribution) and when intervention is warranted.
Concept 2: Mechanisms/tools of intervention (public provision, taxation, subsidies) and how they alter resource allocation and income distribution.
Concept 3: The threefold objectives of public finance (efficient allocation, distribution of income, economic stability) and the cause–effect relationships between interventions and these outcomes.

questions.question: Why can taking on debt up to an optimal point increase a firm's value, even if projects could be funded with equity, and how is that optimal debt level chosen?

options.A: Because debt always reduces corporate taxes for every project, so after-tax cash flows are always higher.
options.B: Because debt removes volatility for equity holders by absorbing all the firm's risk.
options.C: Because interest creates a tax shield that lowers the firm's after-tax cost of capital and raises firm value; but more debt raises expected bankruptcy and agency costs, so the optimal level is where the marginal benefit of the tax shield equals the marginal expected cost of distress and agency costs.
options.D: Because debt guarantees stable earnings by locking in fixed payments.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and wording simplified; kept key ideas: tax shield benefit of debt, rising bankruptcy/agency costs, and the trade-off rule for the optimal point.
concepts_tested: Value maximization through efficient acquisition and deployment of financial resources (short- and long-term) to maximize firm value.
Cash flow management and liquidity as a foundational short-term objective enabling day-to-day operations and survival.
Long-term financing decisions and capital structure (debt vs. equity) and capital budgeting as core mechanisms linking funding decisions to value creation, with ties to corporate finance.

questions.question: How do informal institutions (like norms or handshakes) change the costs and reliability of exchanges, and when do they stop working?

options.A: They always replace formal rules and always lower enforcement and compliance costs.
options.B: They provide perfect, universal enforcement regardless of who is in the group, so formal institutions are unnecessary.
options.C: They lower transaction costs by reducing uncertainty through shared expectations and reputation when actors share beliefs; they fail in heterogeneous or low-trust groups where beliefs diverge, causing coordination failures, higher costs, or reliance on formal enforcement.
options.D: They only affect non-economic social interactions and do not change production or exchange costs.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, removed dense academic phrasing, kept core ideas: reputation/shared expectations lower transaction costs; failure arises in low-trust or heterogeneous groups leading to coordination problems and need for formal rules.
concepts_tested: Institutions as constraints and enablers: how rules and norms shape behavior through shared expectations and enforcement.
Formal vs informal institutions: how varying inclusivity and complexity of definitions reflect different mechanisms and scopes (laws vs. norms, handshakes, etc.).
Economic impact of institutions: how institutional structures influence transaction/production costs and overall economic performance through rights, obligations, and third-party enforcement.

questions.question: A city introduces a formal rule requiring taxi drivers to buy a \$100,000 medallion to operate, while an unwritten norm develops that passengers tip about 20% for good service. Which option best explains how these two institutions shape driver and passenger behavior and the taxi market's economics?

options.A: The medallion (formal) is a legally enforced constraint that raises entry and transaction costs and tends to increase fares and reduce competition; the tipping norm (informal) is socially enforced and creates expectations that motivate better service, so both produce predictable rights, obligations and incentives.
options.B: Both the medallion and the tipping custom are formal institutions because they are widely followed and therefore have equal legal enforceability and economic effects.
options.C: Institutions only restrict behavior: the medallion merely limits drivers' freedom but does not affect fares, service quality, or transaction costs.
options.D: Informal norms like tipping cannot affect economic outcomes; only formal rules such as medallions change costs and market structure.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete taxi market scenario contrasting a formal legal requirement (medallion) with an informal norm (tipping) to test enforcement, rights/obligations, coordination, and effects on transaction/production costs.
concepts_tested: Institutions as socially sanctioned constraints that create rights, obligations and predictable enforcement
Formal vs informal institutions and differences in enforceability and coordination
Institutions' effects on economic performance via transaction and production costs

questions.question: How do rules and norms for property rights and enforcement lower the costs of buying and making things?

options.A: They give perfect information and remove all risk.
options.B: They reduce cheating and uncertainty, so people can trade and specialize with predictable incentives.
options.C: They replace private deals with government orders, causing more bureaucracy and delays.
options.D: They only affect social life, not buying or producing goods.

Concept 2: The formal–informal spectrum of institutions and the role of enforcement in maintaining coordination and compliance (e.g., how varying levels of formality and third-party enforcement affect stability).

Concept 3: Mutually related rights and obligations and collective enforcement as the basis of social order (e.g., how shared expectations and enforcement mechanisms sustain institutional functioning).

questions.question: In a town market, a law makes sellers post prices and families teach children how to bargain. Which choice best shows how institutions coordinate behavior and change trade costs?

options.A: The price rule is a formal institution that makes trade cheaper and easier, and family is a primary institution guiding behavior.
options.B: Institutions are natural laws people are born following with no human design.
options.C: Only money can be an institution; family teaching is not part of institutions.
options.D: Institutions are just private habits and cannot affect production or exchange costs.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short market scenario linking a formal rule and family teaching, then offered options that test how institutions coordinate behavior, affect costs, and include primary institutions.
concepts_tested: Institutions as humanly devised constraints that shape interaction and economic performance
Institutions as collectively enforced expectations (formal and informal) that coordinate behavior
Primary/meta-institutions (family, money) as broad frameworks that organize related institutions

options.A: The legislature always has final control, so executives and courts must follow it.
options.B: Higher rules like a constitution limit all sources; courts can strike down overreach and legislatures can change laws.

questions.question: In a common law system, why do earlier court decisions limit a judge? How can this limit be changed?

questions.question: Why do cultural studies scholars treat culture as changing and process-based rather than fixed and bounded?

options.A: Because culture is a stable set of artifacts and meanings that never change.
options.B: Because one dominant tradition alone determines culture and enforces uniform meanings.
options.C: Because culture arises from ongoing interactions among practices, meanings, institutions, and power relations, so it is continually reconstituted and changed.
options.D: Because culture is unrelated to social structures and only reflects individual tastes.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one clear sentence; options made concise and direct while keeping original meanings; technical idea of interaction among practices and power kept in plain language.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Culture is dynamic and processual, not fixed; cultures are constituted by interacting practices and changing processes.
Concept 2: Culture, power, and meaning: meaning is generated, disseminated, contested, and bound up with social, political, and economic power, involving ideology and hegemony.

Concept 3: Interdisciplinary, theory-rich approach: cultural studies employs multiple frameworks (e.g., Marxism, semiotics, postcolonialism, feminist theory) to analyze cultural phenomena and their relation to broader structures.

questions.question: A new dance from a neighborhood goes viral, parents worry, and school changes rules; which explanation fits cultural studies?

options.B: Culture is made and changed by people; media and power (parents, schools) shape meanings, studied by many fields.
options.C: Only economics explains the dance because money makes trends popular.
options.D: The dance is a natural fact like weather, not linked to politics or power.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete viral-dance scenario to test ideas of culture as dynamic, power shaping meaning, and interdisciplinary study; kept language short for middle school.
concepts_tested: Culture is dynamic and socially constructed
Meaning and power (hegemony/agency) shape cultural practices
Interdisciplinary methods link culture to social, political, and economic contexts

questions.question: How do schools and media help the main culture feel normal to many people?

options.A: People naturally think the same way.
options.B: The state forces one meaning on people with punishments and laws.
options.C: A network of schools, TV, religions, and courts spreads ideas tied to power.
options.D: Global technology makes cultures very similar everywhere.

Concept 2: Culture as dynamic and processual (cultures are interacting, changing sets of practices rather than fixed entities).

Concept 3: Interdisciplinary frameworks and mechanisms (use of multiple theoretical approaches including Marxism, semiotics, feminist theory, postcolonialism; ideas like cultural hegemony and agency; globalization as a context for cultural analysis).

questions.question: How do innate immune cells start adaptive T cell responses, and how do innate sensing signals shape what type of T cells develop?

options.A: They display peptide–MHC complexes to T cells (antigen presentation), and innate-derived co-stimulatory signals plus cytokines from sensors like PRRs (pattern-recognition receptors) determine T cell activation and differentiation.
options.B: Antigen presentation by innate cells directly activates B cells and triggers antibody production without involving T cells.
options.C: Antigen presentation only happens after the adaptive system is already active, so innate signals do not influence T cell fate.
options.D: Strong MHC–TCR binding alone always activates naive T cells; co-stimulatory signals and cytokines are not required.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened to two clear sentences; kept key terms (antigen presentation, MHC, PRRs) and added brief parenthetical explanations; answer choices made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Innate immunity mechanisms and their role in activating the adaptive immune system via antigen presentation.
Concept 2: The physical and chemical barriers (skin, mucosa, mucus, peristalsis, cilia, tears, gut flora) as the first line of defense and the mechanisms by which they prevent infection.
Concept 3: Epithelial barrier theory and the idea that barrier dysfunction, environmental exposures, and microbial dysbiosis can drive inflammation and contribute to chronic diseases.

questions.question: Why do highly strained molecules usually relax to less strained conformations?

options.A: Because strain energy comes from geometric constraints that weaken bonds (like a compressed spring); moving to a less strained shape lowers internal energy (mainly enthalpy) and Gibbs free energy $\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T\Delta S^{\circ}$, so the change is spontaneous and the equilibrium follows $K_{\rm eq}=\exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}\right)$.
options.B: Because strain energy strengthens bonds in the strained conformation, so the molecule cannot relax to other forms.
options.C: Because entropy always dominates and forces the system toward maximum disorder, regardless of bond strain or enthalpy.
options.D: Because external pressure adds or removes energy and that alone forces the molecule to change conformation.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one clear sentence for high-school level; kept core thermodynamic idea and included the Gibbs equation and Keq in option A; simplified and shortened distractors.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Strain energy arises from structural constraints that weaken bonds, effectively storing energy in a molecule (like a compressed spring) and influencing which conformations are energetically favorable.

Concept 2: The stability of molecular conformations is governed by Gibbs free energy differences, with ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS°, and the equilibrium constant Keq = exp(−ΔG°/(RT)), linking structure, enthalpy, entropy, and spontaneity.
Concept 3: Highly strained (high-energy) conformations tend to spontaneously relax to lower-energy conformations, illustrating a fundamental tendency toward thermodynamic stability.

questions.question: Which sentence best shows how people and places affect each other over time?

options.A: Environmental determinism says the environment fully shapes people and people cannot change places.
options.B: Spaces shape daily choices, and many small choices change the space, which then changes future choices.
options.C: People only create spaces through culture, with no influence from the environment.
options.D: Natural changes to space happen first, and people only follow those changes passively.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used simpler words, and made each option brief while keeping the feedback loop idea and contrasting environmental determinism.
concepts_tested: The bidirectional relationship between people and places: how human activity shapes spaces and how spaces/environments influence people.
Interdisciplinary, mixed-methods approach to studying human-environment interactions (qualitative and quantitative methods).
Environmental determinism as a historical framework linking environment to human behavior, along with noted critiques and limitations.

questions.question: Culture is learned, not genetic. Why do people in different places make different tools, customs, and rules even though human minds are similar?

options.B: Because culture is learned and passed on, so groups adapt quickly without genetic change.
options.C: Because all human groups have the same practices, so environment cannot explain differences.
options.D: Because environmental effects are random and do not shape cultural choices.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the idea into two short sentences, removed jargon, used simple phrases like "learned" and "passed on" while keeping the core idea that culture allows rapid local adaptation without genetic change.

Concept 2: The tension/relationship between local particular cultures and global connections (universal human nature or web of connections).
Concept 3: Foundational theoretical frameworks (cultural relativism and historical particularism) guiding interpretation of cultural variation.

questions.question: Why can adding road capacity fail to reduce congestion long-term and make environmental impacts worse?

options.A: It lowers travel time and money cost for all trips, causing a permanent fall in congestion.
options.B: Induced demand: lower generalized cost (money + time) attracts extra trips and longer trips, raising total vehicle‑kilometres traveled (VKT) and increasing congestion and emissions.

Induced demand and the environmental/congestion implications of capacity expansion
Externalities of transport (positive and negative), including congestion and climate change impacts

questions.question: A text editor calls “save” to write a file while a browser and music player run; which sequence correctly shows the OS acting as an abstraction, using scheduling, system calls, and interrupts?

options.A: The application issues a system call to the kernel to write the file; the kernel initiates disk I/O (often via DMA) and schedules other processes. When the disk finishes it raises a hardware interrupt, the kernel's interrupt handler runs, and the kernel resumes the saving process.

options.C: The application makes a system call and the kernel blocks and busy-waits, preventing other programs from running until the disk write completes.
options.D: The kernel copies the file into its own memory and completes the write without using interrupts or scheduling other processes.

OS as abstraction/kernel mediating between applications and hardware
Interaction mechanisms: system calls to request services and hardware interrupts triggering ISR

questions.question: Consider three species: Species A has exclusive maternal care, Species B has exclusive paternal care, and Species C has equal biparental care. According to parental investment theory and parent–offspring conflict, which set of predictions is correct?

Modes of Parental Care and Mating Systems (exclusive maternal, exclusive paternal, biparental effects)

questions.question: A multinational company trains a supplier in Bangladesh to design and brand parts while assembly and distribution happen in other countries; later, local makers use open-source 3D printing to make similar parts near customers. Which statement best describes the likely effects on the supplier and the global value chain (GVC)?

options.A: The supplier can achieve functional upgrading (moving into design and branding) via transferred technology and skills; this case shows a GVC spans design, production and postproduction across borders; 3D printing could localize some production and partly disrupt the chain.
options.B: The supplier will automatically own the entire global chain after training, and 3D printing will make no change to where value is created.
options.C: This is merely a supply chain that only moves materials, so design transfer is irrelevant; 3D printing will strengthen global specialization.
options.D: GVCs include only production stages, not design or marketing, and upgrading occurs only through tariffs rather than technology transfer.

GVC as cross-border network of design, production and postproduction (distinct from simple supply chain)
localization/disruption of GVCs by open-source 3D printing and digital fabrication

questions.question: If two people have the same genetic risk and face the same stress, why might only one develop a mental health problem?

questions.question: A lab finds a new drug that repairs cells. Which sequence best follows benchside → bedside → community and overcomes both T1 and T2 roadblocks?

T1 and T2 translational roadblocks
interdisciplinary steps to move discoveries into public health practice

questions.question: Why does generalizing input test cases help make software more robust, and how does testing confirm that the generalization works?

options.A: Generalization cuts the inputs down to a few cases so you can test every case completely; testing then proves those few cover all cases.
options.B: Generalization chooses a representative set of inputs that capture likely variations so the program can handle unseen similar inputs; testing (like fuzz testing and fault injection) uses varied and unexpected inputs to check for graceful behavior and find weaknesses the generalized set missed.
options.C: Generalization increases code complexity, so you need less testing because the chosen family already covers all edge cases.
options.D: Generalization guarantees the system will work for any input, making testing unnecessary.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer, used simpler phrasing, kept key ideas: representative test sets, role of fuzz/fault-injection testing, and finding weaknesses.
concepts_tested: Generalization of input cases as a design principle to improve robustness and the role of testing in validating it
Redundancy and software diversity as mechanisms to tolerate failures, including why blind code addition is harmful and how equivalent functionality enables failure replacement
Interaction between robustness, evolvability/adaptability, and testing techniques (how testing demonstrates robustness and supports adaptable design)

questions.question: A hospital's automated drug‑delivery robot must deliver each dose within 5 seconds and remain trustworthy even if software bugs or network attacks occur. Using the Attributes–Threats–Means framework, which choice correctly names (1) an attribute to measure, (2) a threat type, and (3) an appropriate means to improve the robot's dependability?

options.A: Attribute: Reliability — continuity of correct service within the 5 s deadline; Threat: faults or attacks that break timing; Means: fault tolerance (redundancy) plus security controls to keep service on time.
options.B: Attribute: Maintainability — ease of repair after a problem; Threat: observed failures at the system boundary; Means: forecasting future faults by log analysis.
options.C: Attribute: Safety — absence of catastrophic harm to the patient; Threat: internal errors that are always visible; Means: prevention via coding standards.
options.D: Attribute: Availability — readiness for correct service; Threat: permanent hardware faults only; Means: removal during development through verification.

questions.generation_notes: Created a real-world time‑bounded scenario and mapped each option to the Attributes–Threats–Means model; only A links time‑bound reliability with faults/attacks and appropriate means (tolerance + security).
concepts_tested: Attributes–Threats–Means framework
Inclusion of safety and security within dependability
Time‑bounded trustworthy service in real‑time systems (faults and attacks)

questions.question: A robot moves on a doughnut-shaped track (a torus); scientists study all smooth motion plans and search for shortest paths. What area of math studies these global behaviors using spaces of mappings and handles sudden changes (singularities)?

options.A: Global analysis — studies motions on manifolds, spaces of mappings, singularities, and shortest paths
options.B: Euclidean geometry — studies flat shapes and simple distances on planes
options.C: Algebraic number theory — studies properties of whole numbers and equations
options.D: Calculus of variations — finds shortest paths but not the full topological behavior of mappings

questions.generation_notes: Used a robot-on-a-torus scenario to link manifolds, spaces of mappings, singularities, and finding shortest paths to test global analysis vs related fields.
concepts_tested: Global/topological properties of differential equations on manifolds
Use of spaces of mappings and singularities (catastrophe theory) to classify behaviors
Overlap with optimization/variational principles (geodesics) and applications to physics

questions.question: Why does $N^*N = NN^*$ let N act like multiplication on some $L^2$ space?

options.A: Normal operators always have a short list of special vectors.
options.B: Normality makes N act like multiplication on each part of the space.
options.C: We can change any operator bit by bit to look diagonal.
options.D: Normal operators only have point numbers in their spectrum.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I showed how normality lets N act by multiplication.
concepts_tested: Spectral theorem: conditions under which an operator can be diagonalized or represented by multiplication operators; connection to commutative C*-algebras and spectral decomposition.
Normal operators: definition via commutation with the adjoint (NN* = N*N) and their central role because the spectral theorem applies to them.

Representation and dimensionality: how diagonalization and spectral decomposition differ between finite-dimensional spaces and infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, including the idea of modeling operators via multiplication.

questions.question: During high brain (cortical) activity, how does the neurovascular unit increase local blood flow to meet energy needs?

options.A: Action potentials travel through the blood and directly depolarize vessel muscle, causing dilation.
options.B: Neurotransmitters simply diffuse to vessel walls and cause a rapid, permanent widening of the vessels.
options.C: Neurons and astrocytes (a type of glial cell) release vasoactive signals at active synapses that act on nearby arterioles to transiently dilate them and increase blood flow.
options.D: Local blood flow is fixed and only changes with systemic blood pressure, not with local neuronal activity.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used plain wording, explained 'astrocytes' briefly, and made all four options concise but plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Neurons transmit signals via electrochemical impulses along axons, using electrical synapses or chemical synapses with neurotransmitters to influence other cells.

Concept 2: The nervous system is organized into CNS and PNS, with divisions such as somatic vs autonomic (and autonomic’s sympathetic, parasympathetic, and enteric) that coordinate different states and actions.

Concept 3: Neural circuits, networks, and the neurovascular unit illustrate how structure and signaling dynamics generate perception and behavior while meeting energy demands through regulated cerebral blood flow.

questions.question: A soccer player sees a ball and the brain tells arm muscles to catch it. Which option lists the nerve types used, the support cell for peripheral nerves, and the muscle neurotransmitter?

options.A: Sensory (afferent) then motor (efferent); Schwann cells; acetylcholine
options.B: Motor then sensory; oligodendrocytes; dopamine
options.C: Sensory then motor; astrocytes; GABA
options.D: Motor then sensory; Schwann cells; glutamate

structural divisions (CNS vs PNS pathways, nerve types)
support cells and integration (Schwann cells myelinate PNS axons; role of glia)

questions.question: Why can a lesion in a peripheral nerve cause muscle weakness even when the brain's motor circuits are intact?

options.A: Because central motor commands are generated and executed entirely inside the brain, so peripheral damage should not stop movement.
options.B: Because motor commands originate in the CNS but must travel along peripheral nerves to reach muscles; a lesion in the peripheral nerve blocks that transmission and causes weakness.
options.C: Because peripheral nerve lesions affect only sensory signals, leaving motor function normal.
options.D: Because the CNS and PNS work independently for movement, so damage to one does not affect the other.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one clear sentence and options rewritten concisely to focus on CNS→PNS signal transmission and the effect of peripheral lesions; kept original correct choice.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The CNS vs PNS organization as a framework for understanding nervous system connectivity and function.
Concept 2: Lesion studies as a method for inferring the relationship between brain structure and behavior/function.
Concept 3: Comparative neuroanatomy: how bilateral symmetry leads to segregated, better-understood nervous systems compared to radial symmetry.

questions.question: Why do neural circuits (neurons plus glia, which are helper cells) produce new functions not seen in single neurons?

options.A: Because a single neuron can do any complex brain job by itself.
options.B: Because complex, changing connections and signals among cells create new patterns only when they act together.
options.C: Because glial helper cells do most electrical signaling, so neuron properties don't matter.
options.D: Because adding more neurons just averages differences, so no new behaviors appear.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, used one clear question sentence, defined glia in parentheses, and simplified technical terms (nonlinear, topology, plasticity) to 'complex, changing connections and signals'.
concepts_tested: Emergent properties of neural circuits: how complex functions arise from interactions among neurons and glia beyond individual components.

Levels of analysis and cross-scale integration: studying the nervous system from molecular/cellular scales to imaging and cognitive tasks, and how each scale contributes to understanding brain function.

Multidisciplinary framework and scope: the role of integrating physiology, anatomy, molecular biology, psychology, physics, computer science, and more to understand learning, memory, behavior, perception, and consciousness.

questions.question: Why do learning and feelings come from many brain cells instead of one?

options.A: Each brain cell acts alone to form the whole.
options.B: Many brain cells work together to make new patterns.
options.C: Only chemical signals at synapses store information.
options.D: Learning does not change after birth.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences to explain teamwork of brain cells.
concepts_tested: Emergence: How complex properties of brains and behavior arise from interactions among neurons, glia, and neural circuits.
Multiscale/integrative approach: Connecting molecular, cellular, system, and cognitive levels using diverse disciplines and techniques.
Brain–behavior relationships: The links between neural mechanisms and learning, memory, perception, behavior, and consciousness.

questions.question: Why does giving health resources based on need (rather than giving everyone the same resources) better promote health equity?

options.A: It directs more resources to people with the greatest need, where each extra resource produces the largest health gain, helping close gaps with better-off groups.
options.B: It gives everyone identical health inputs, aiming for equal treatment but not correcting existing disadvantages.
options.C: It assumes health is fixed by genetics and cannot be improved by reallocating resources.
options.D: It sacrifices overall population health to maximize fairness, lowering total health outcomes.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, clarified “marginal health benefit” as “each extra resource produces the largest health gain,” and kept the equity vs equality distinction; answer choices made concise and plausible.

Concept 2: Social determinants of health and SES as mechanisms linking access to resources with health outcomes (relationship between wealth/power/prestige and health disparities).
Concept 3: Health as a human right and the development/justice framework (principle linking health equity to human rights and to Millennium Development Goals).

questions.question: Why does control of maritime chokepoints often give a country more geopolitical power even if it is not the largest or most populous state?

options.A: Because it lets the country project cultural or ideological influence across the world.

Geopolitics as a methodological approach: Using geographic variables to understand, explain, and predict foreign policy and state behavior.
Critical geopolitics and power/ideology: How geopolitical theories themselves reflect political power and ideological aims, prompting deconstruction of traditional doctrines.

questions.question: Barnett and Duvall define power as "the production, in and through social relations, of effects that shape the capacities of actors to determine their circumstances and fate." Why does this definition imply that persuasion (convincing someone) is not itself constitutive of power?

options.A: Persuasion changes beliefs but does not alter the social relations or structural capacities that produce power effects, so it does not create the lasting outcomes that define power.
options.B: Persuasion always uses coercive threats, which would make it a form of power rather than exclude it.
options.C: Persuasion directly increases an actor's material resources, and material resources are the only source of power in this framework.
options.D: Persuasion is just a neutral mental process and thus cannot be analyzed as part of the social relations that produce power.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the stem to one sentence, clarified 'persuasion' as 'convincing someone', simplified option wording while keeping four plausible answers and retaining the original correct choice.
concepts_tested: Material power vs. relational power and their interplay in shaping outcomes
Power as production in social relations (Barnett & Duvall) and why persuasion is not constitutive of power
The taxonomy of political power (power as a goal, influence/control, victory/security, resources, status) and implications for state behavior

questions.question: How can a group's power grow without more money or weapons?

options.C: They convince people with words.
options.D: They get more money or weapons to gain power.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced big words with simple words.

Concept 2: Polarity (unipolar, bipolar, multipolar) as a distribution of power in the international system and its effects on state behavior and inter-state dynamics.

Concept 3: Multiple conceptualizations of power (goal of states/leaders, influence/control outcomes, victory/security, resource/control, status) and the methodological implication that power is production of effects through social relations (as argued by Barnett & Duvall), not mere causality or persuasion.

questions.question: Country A (core) makes high-tech goods and earns large profits. Country B (periphery) exports raw materials cheaply and stays poor over decades; which explanation best fits world-systems theory?

questions.question: A city debates a ban on single-use plastics: newspapers emphasize economic costs, a viral social media post falsely claims prices will double, and local celebrities post pro-ban videos. Which choice best explains how public opinion is likely formed, how it can affect whether the ban passes, and how researchers should measure opinion given the misinformation?

options.A: News outlets set the agenda and frame the ban as costly (agenda-setting and framing); social media misinformation creates active misinformed and pseudo-opinions and social conformity (social desirability) can mask true views; influentials (celebrities) persuade by testimonial; public opinion can legitimize or constrain policymakers; researchers should combine representative surveys with sentiment analysis and fact-checking to adjust for misinformation.
options.B: Influencers alone decide public opinion, so celebrities will determine the vote; policymakers ignore newspapers and social media, and simple polls that ask one question are sufficient to measure opinion.
options.C: Public opinion comes only from deep-held values, so media framing and misinformation do not matter; measurement should rely on a single national poll because opinions are stable.
options.D: Misinformation makes it impossible to know public opinion, so no measurement method like surveys or sentiment analysis can be trusted and policymakers should disregard public views.

questions.generation_notes: Created a concrete city policy scenario to link media agenda-setting, framing, influentials, social conformity, policy legitimacy, and measurement methods (surveys + sentiment analysis) while highlighting misinformation challenges.
concepts_tested: media effects on opinion formation (agenda-setting and framing)
opinion as a driver/constraint on governance and social conformity (legitimacy, social desirability)
methods to study public opinion (surveys, sentiment analysis) and the impact of misinformation

questions.question: Why is earned media often seen as more credible than paid media, and how does control over placement and exposure differ between them?

options.A: Earned media is made by the organization and has guaranteed placement, so its controlled message makes it more credible.
options.B: Earned media comes from independent third parties without direct payment, acting as a third-party endorsement that boosts credibility; organizations have less control over when and where it appears, while paid media gives guaranteed placement and timing but can seem promotional and less trustworthy.
options.C: Earned media always reaches a larger audience than paid media, and that larger reach is the only reason it is more credible.
options.D: Earned and paid media are equally credible; the only difference is how much budget each requires.

Concept 3: Media relations and stakeholder targeting as mechanisms to inform and persuade audiences to maintain a positive organizational perception

questions.question: Three organisms: (1) a human who eats enough calories but never eats citrus fruit; (2) a tomato plant grown in soil with potassium and phosphorus but no available nitrogen; (3) a bacterium that oxidizes iron(II) for energy. Which statement correctly predicts the nutrition-related outcomes for all three?

options.C: Lack of citrus only reduces sugar intake with no health effects; the plant will fix atmospheric nitrogen to compensate; the bacterium is a phototroph that uses light.
options.D: The human’s main problem will be lack of essential amino acids; the plant’s primary issue is phosphorus deficiency; the bacterium is an auxotroph that must consume preformed organic nutrients.

questions.generation_notes: Created a concrete three-part scenario to test macronutrient vs micronutrient roles, organism-specific nutrient acquisition (plant nitrate uptake, bacterial lithotrophy), and health/growth outcomes of deficiencies.

Organism-specific nutrient acquisition strategies (plants, bacteria, humans)
Health outcomes from nutrient deficiency vs adequacy (scurvy, nitrogen-limited growth, chemolithotrophy)

questions.question: Task A and Task B happen at the same time. Why does paying more attention to Task A make Task B worse?

questions.question: Why do people have only so much attention? How does this change doing many tasks at once?

questions.question: A toy company uses cloud servers, runs an online order system, sells toys on its website, and adds sensors to toys that connect to apps. questions.question: Why does reaching a critical mass make an innovation self-sustaining, and which mechanism best explains the shift from early adopters to mass uptake?
