Concept 2: Feedback loops and epigenetic/chromatin modifications contribute to cellular memory and stable cell identity during development.
Concept 3: Morphogen gradients and diffusion of gene products provide positional information that guides tissue patterning and cell fate decisions.

questions.question: How does using several transcription factors together let a gene be turned on only in certain cells or conditions?

options.A: One TF binding is enough to recruit the transcription machinery, so extra TFs only add redundancy.
options.B: The gene's regulatory region integrates signals from several TFs; only a specific combination recruits coactivators and alters chromatin so transcription can occur, giving tissue- or condition-specific expression.
options.C: Transcription happens only if every possible TF is bound at once, so expression is independent of cellular context.
options.D: TFs mainly compete for the same sites, so expression depends on which TF wins the competition, not on a specific combination.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clearer wording, kept technical terms like 'coactivators' and 'chromatin' with simple context; options made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: DNA-binding domains define target recognition and TF classification; TFs regulate genes by binding specific DNA sequences adjacent to the genes they control.

Concept 2: Activation vs. repression mechanisms; TFs influence transcription by promoting or blocking RNA polymerase recruitment, often via interactions with coactivators and chromatin-modifying proteins.
Concept 3: Combinatorial regulation; multiple transcription factors bind to a gene’s regulatory regions, enabling context-dependent, coordinated control of gene expression.

questions.question: How does morphogenesis form the three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm? Why can't tissue growth by cell proliferation do this alone?

options.A: It uses coordinated cell movements and tissue remodeling to rearrange cells into layers, while growth only increases size.
options.B: By just making the tissue bigger so different regions spontaneously become the three layers without any cell movement.
options.C: By regional specification alone from cytoplasmic determinants, so no cell rearrangement is needed.
options.D: By only inductive signals that set layer identities, without any mechanical or positional reorganization.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex sentence into two short sentences, used simpler words, and kept each option concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Regional specification establishes spatial patterning through cytoplasmic determinants and inductive signals, guiding later differentiation.
Concept 2: Morphogenesis drives three-dimensional shaping via coordinated cell movements and tissue remodeling, enabling germ layer formation and organ development.
Concept 3: Tissue growth and allometry contribute to morphogenesis through proliferation, cell size changes, and extracellular matrix deposition, affecting overall shape and structure.

questions.question: A scientist paints one side of a frog egg with a special chemical. What made cells near the paint become head cells?

options.A: The chemical told nearby cells to make head parts.
options.B: Nearby cells moved together to make a head shape.
options.C: Cells on that side grew faster to make head parts.
options.D: Random chance picked those cells to be head cells.

questions.generation_notes: Used a painted egg to test regional signals.
concepts_tested: regional signals set cell identity
cell identity versus cell movement and growth

questions.question: How do cells use a smooth signal to make different cell types?

options.A: Each cell reads the exact signal level to make a matching cell type.
options.B: Cells compare the signal to set limits to start programs, reinforced by neighbor signals.
options.C: The signal pushes cells into stripes without changing genes.
options.D: Cells choose cell types randomly when the signal fades.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to keep the threshold idea clear. I kept the idea that neighbors sharpen borders.
concepts_tested: Morphogen gradient–based positional information (e.g., French flag model) guiding cell fates along a field
Reaction–diffusion (Turing) mechanisms generating patterns through chemical interactions and diffusion
Alternative/mechanical and computational patterning frameworks (elastic instability, cellular automata, neural networks) as models to explain or simulate patterns

questions.question: Which choice correctly describes phase boundaries, the triple point, the critical point, and the solidus/liquidus on a phase diagram?

options.A: Phase boundaries mark the P–T conditions where two or more phases coexist in equilibrium; crossing one produces a phase transition. The triple point is a single P–T where three phases meet; the critical point ends the liquid–gas boundary so above it liquid and gas are indistinguishable; the solidus and liquidus give temperatures below which the substance is fully solid, above which it is fully liquid, and between which a solid–liquid mixture exists.
options.B: The triple point is a range of pressures and temperatures where three phases can coexist, not a single point. The critical point is where the solid and liquid become indistinguishable.
options.C: Crossing a phase boundary only changes the speed of molecules and does not change which phase is present. Solidus and liquidus are the temperatures at which vapor first appears.
options.D: The liquid–gas phase boundary continues without end and never terminates. Between the solidus and liquidus the material is a pure liquid.

questions.generation_notes: Created a single multiple-choice question that asks students to identify correct definitions for phase boundaries, triple point, critical point, and solidus/liquidus; distractors each contain a common misconception about one or two features.
concepts_tested: Phase boundaries and equilibrium coexistence
Triple point vs critical point
Solidus and liquidus and the mixed (mushy) region

questions.question: Two cells that have the same DNA can become different cell types. Which mechanism best explains how each cell controls which genes are turned on (expressed)?

options.A: Each cell gets different DNA mutations over time, so they end up with different genes.
options.B: Each cell destroys most RNA transcripts so only a single gene's message remains and is expressed.
options.C: Cells change DNA accessibility (chromatin remodeling) and use different transcription factor networks, so different sets of genes are transcribed in each cell type.
options.D: Cells translate the same mRNA at different rates without changing which genes are transcribed, producing different amounts of protein.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used 'turned on' for 'expressed', and explained chromatin remodeling and transcription factors briefly; answer choices shortened to ≤2 sentences and kept biologically plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Evolution by natural selection as the driver of biological diversity and adaptation (how changes in populations over time lead to new traits and species).
Concept 2: The cell as the basic unit of life and genes/heredity as the basis of inheritance (how cellular organization and DNA encode, transmit, and express biological information).

Concept 3: Energy transformation and homeostasis as unifying mechanisms (how organisms obtain, transform, and use energy and maintain internal stability, and how energy flow/nutrient cycling support life across ecosystems).

questions.question: How does natural selection make a population change over time?

options.A: Allele frequencies change by random chance, independent of fitness.
options.B: Mating choices change genotype ratios, causing helpful alleles to rise.
options.C: Individuals with heritable traits that help survival and reproduction leave more offspring, so those traits increase.
options.D: Beneficial mutations occur often and quickly replace less fit alleles.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and simplified wording; options kept short and clear; original distractors (genetic drift, nonrandom mating, mutation) preserved; correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Evolution by natural selection as the driver of biological diversity and a unifying framework for biology
Concept 2: The cell as the basic unit of life and the role of genes/heredity in inheritance (cellular and genetic basis of life)
Concept 3: Energy transformation and homeostasis as core life processes that govern organism function and ecosystem energy flow

questions.question: A 70-year-old's skin cells have many UV-induced pyrimidine dimers (DNA lesions). Which statement best explains why these lesions increase the person's risk of aging and of developing skin cancer?

options.A: The DNA damage response attempts to repair pyrimidine dimers, but older skin repairs less efficiently so lesions accumulate, blocking transcription or causing mutations in critical genes; failure of repair can lead to senescence, apoptosis, or uncontrolled cell division (tumor).
options.B: Because repair enzymes always restore the exact DNA sequence, pyrimidine dimers only cause short transcription delays and do not increase cancer or aging risk.
options.C: UV-induced pyrimidine dimers mainly damage mitochondrial DNA and therefore only reduce ATP production, without affecting nuclear gene expression or cancer risk.
options.D: Aging raises DNA repair activity so older skin avoids mutations; accumulated damage produces only harmless benign growths, not cancer.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short clinical scenario (UV lesions in aged skin) and wrote one correct option linking DDR, reduced repair efficiency with age/environment, transcription disruption, and outcomes (senescence/apoptosis/cancer); other options are plausible misconceptions.
concepts_tested: DNA damage response and outcomes (senescence, apoptosis, tumor formation)
Effect of cell type/age/environment on DNA repair efficiency and resulting aging/cancer risk
How DNA lesions disrupt transcription and cause mutations in critical genes leading to tumors

questions.question: Two cells suffer DNA damage of equal number: (1) an older skin cell with reduced DNA repair capacity that retains an unrepaired lesion in a tumor suppressor gene, and (2) a young cell with strong repair capacity that has lesions in noncoding DNA. Which outcome is most likely?

options.A: The older skin cell is more likely to develop cancer because poor repair plus a lesion in a critical gene increases cancer risk.
options.B: The young cell is more likely to develop cancer because younger cells always divide and accumulate mutations faster.
options.C: Both cells will immediately undergo apoptosis because any DNA damage triggers programmed cell death.
options.D: Neither cell is affected because noncoding DNA damage cannot cause problems.

questions.generation_notes: Wrote a one-sentence, concrete scenario contrasting repair capacity, cell age/type, and lesion location; options test outcomes (cancer, apoptosis, false absolutes).
concepts_tested: DNA repair preserves genome integrity and affects cancer risk
Effect of cell type/age and lesion location on repair outcome (senescence/apoptosis/unregulated division)

questions.question: How do large-scale data collection and analytics tools change how a team makes decisions during and before games?

options.A: They only increase the amount of data but do not change decision criteria.
options.B: They let teams measure uncertainty explicitly and run simulations of alternative strategies so they can compare choices before acting.
options.C: They guarantee finding the best possible play in every situation.
options.D: They fully replace a coach's intuition with automated recommendations.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified the question; kept technical idea that analytics quantify uncertainty and enable simulations; made all options concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Data collection and technology enable analytics (advanced statistics, machine learning, simulations) and drive decision-making.
On-field analytics vs off-field analytics represent distinct yet related mechanisms with different objectives (performance optimization vs business growth).
Analytics influence decision-making and outcomes (competitive advantage as in Moneyball; impact on betting and fan engagement).

questions.question: Why is analyzing your audience important for good public speaking, and how do you adapt when the listeners are diverse?

options.A: Because it finds one set of facts that will persuade everyone, and adaptability just means speaking louder so everyone can hear.
options.B: Because it aligns your content with the audience's knowledge, values, and interests; adaptability means tailoring examples, language, structure, and delivery (tone, pace, and media) to fit different listeners.
options.C: Because it removes the need for humor, and adaptability means switching topics mid-speech to follow audience reactions.
options.D: Because audience analysis is unnecessary; adaptability just means adding more slides no matter who is listening.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options to plain high-school language while keeping the core idea that audience analysis guides content and adaptability means tailoring examples, language, structure, and delivery.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The relationship between speech purpose (inform vs. influence) and how content/delivery are designed, including how audience affects outcomes.
Concept 2: The centrality of audience analysis and adaptability—recognizing audience diversity and tailoring the speech accordingly.
Concept 3: The role of rhetorical mechanisms (storytelling, informational approaches, visual aids) in achieving aims and how their use depends on context and audience.

questions.question: Why can shotgun metagenomics tell both which microbes are present and what functions they might perform, unlike targeted amplicon methods?

options.A: Because it sequences all DNA in the sample, capturing both taxonomic marker genes and the full set of coding genes for enzymes and pathways.
options.B: Because it only amplifies the 16S rRNA gene and a few metabolic genes to infer function.
options.C: Because it requires culturing each organism first to obtain complete genomes for sequencing.
options.D: Because it sequences only RNA transcripts to show which genes are active.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; technical contrast kept (all DNA vs targeted amplicon); options made concise and clearly connected to methods (amplicon, culturing, RNA).
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Metagenomics as a culture-independent approach that profiles both microbial composition and functional potential from environmental DNA.
Concept 2: The methodological distinction and relational outcomes between metagenomics and amplicon sequencing (data types, ecological insights).
Concept 3: The role of technology and economics (e.g., sequencing costs, shotgun vs long-read sequencing) in enabling large-scale, ecosystem- and health-relevant metagenomic research.

questions.question: Researchers sample two salamander populations separated by a river. They measure microsatellite allele sizes and find large differences with a high FST, and they use next-generation sequencing of soil 16S rRNA to show the bacterial communities on both banks are very similar. Which interpretation best follows from these molecular ecology results?

options.A: The salamanders show genetic differentiation and low gene flow (microsatellite allele-size differences and high FST indicate population structure), while similar 16S profiles mean bacteria are not limited by the river; these molecular methods inform conservation and biogeography.
options.B: The salamanders have high gene flow because allele sizes differ, and the similar bacterial communities mean dispersal is prevented by the river.
options.C: Next-generation sequencing of 16S proves the salamanders are different species, so the microsatellite data are unnecessary.
options.D: A high FST always means the two salamander populations are separate species and no conservation measures are needed.

questions.generation_notes: Created a concrete sampling scenario linking microsatellite allele sizes and FST to gene flow and population structure, and next-generation 16S sequencing to bacterial community analysis; options test correct interpretation and common misconceptions.
concepts_tested: use of allele sizes and FST to infer population structure and gene flow
PCR/sequencing (next-generation) allows study of environmental microbes without culturing
how molecular methods inform conservation, biogeography, and ecological interpretation

questions.question: If power is about social relationships and how it is shared, why can one policy make some people more powerful and others less? How would that show up at micro levels (households, workplaces) and macro levels (institutions, the state)?

options.A: Because power is a fixed resource held by elites; reforms only move that fixed stock around, so you would see similar changes in big indicators but little change in everyday interactions.
options.B: Because power comes from social relations and the ability to influence others; a reform changes actors' relative bargaining positions, so you would see new negotiation patterns in households and workplaces (micro) and shifts in institutional authority or policy enforcement (macro).
options.C: Because power is only about economic wealth; reforms just change who has money, so effects show in macro economic measures but not in day-to-day social relations.
options.D: Because power is purely cultural; reforms change norms and beliefs, so everyday interactions change but formal institutions and their authority stay the same.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question two short sentences, defined micro/macro with examples, and rewrote each option into concise, plausible high-school level statements while keeping the original alternatives.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Interdependence of society and politics — understanding power requires studying both together rather than as separate, assumed variables.
Concept 2: Power as a relational, distributive phenomenon — power is the ability to influence or control, with distribution and change observable across micro to macro levels.
Concept 3: Power structures across sites — analysis of how power operates and is contested from intimate settings (families) to broad institutions (the state).

questions.question: If the activation energy $E_a$ does not change, why does raising the temperature usually make a chemical reaction faster?

options.A: Because higher temperature raises the average kinetic energy of molecules, so a larger fraction of collisions have energy above $E_a$ and cause reaction.
options.B: Because higher temperature lowers the activation energy $E_a$, making it easier for collisions to succeed.
options.C: Because heating shifts the reaction equilibrium toward products, which speeds up the observed rate.
options.D: Because higher temperature reduces the total number of collisions, but each collision is more energetic, so the rate increases.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence and technical terms kept (activation energy $E_a$, collisions). Answer choices made concise and plausible; removed extra discussion of mechanisms and catalysts.
concepts_tested: Activation energy and its role in determining reaction rate and the effect of temperature on rates
Reaction mechanism: sequence of elementary steps, and the idea of a rate-determining step
Catalysis and enzymes: how catalysts accelerate reactions and enable biological pathways

questions.question: In distance education, why is timely feedback from instructors important for effective learning?

options.A: It shortens the gap between doing work and getting evaluation, so students can correct mistakes quickly and manage their own learning when teacher and student are separated.
options.B: Because feedback mainly acts as a general motivator and is not linked to specific learning goals.
options.C: Because giving quick feedback mostly increases course length by requiring more instructor time.
options.D: Because feedback only matters for final grades and does not affect ongoing study.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence, used direct language; kept option A's core idea about rapid correction and self-regulation when instructors and students are apart; other options made concise but plausible distractors.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Feedback as a crucial mechanism that enables effective learning in distance education (e.g., timely feedback compensating for lack of in-person interaction).
Concept 2: Technology- and infrastructure-driven scalability and access (e.g., how mailing systems enabled early distance education and how later technologies (video, TV, Internet) expanded reach).
Concept 3: Interactivity and multiple learning modalities as core elements of effective distance/e-learning (e.g., the need for interactive processes and varied learning modes to support learners).

questions.question: Why do firms often cluster in the same place (economies of agglomeration), and how does being close help with shared inputs and knowledge spillovers?

options.A: Proximity cuts transport and search costs, gives access to specialized suppliers and a common labor pool, and makes knowledge spillovers and faster learning easier, raising productivity.
options.B: Proximity mainly raises rivalry so firms lose market power, which cancels out any clustering benefits.
options.C: Clustering happens only because of government zoning and incentives, not because of market forces or being close to other firms.
options.D: Being near other firms does not change information flow or access to inputs; clustered firms do no better than isolated firms.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, defined 'economies of agglomeration' as firm clustering, and kept concise options that state the main mechanisms (lower costs, shared suppliers/labor, knowledge spillovers) versus alternative explanations.
concepts_tested: Economies of agglomeration and linkages: why clustering of firms/industries occurs and how proximity creates advantages (e.g., shared inputs, knowledge spillovers, reduced transport costs).

Core-periphery dynamics and spatial inequality: how core regions concentrate activity and growth, while peripheries lag, including mechanisms like trade patterns, investment flows, and cumulative causation.

Environment–economy–urban form relationships: how environmental factors and urban form shape economic activity and development, and how economic activity, in turn, affects environmental resources and landscapes.

questions.question: A bank pools \$100 million of consumer loans into a securitization and issues three tranches: senior, mezzanine, and junior. Which explanation best explains how securitization can lower the bank's funding cost and why the senior tranche typically receives a higher credit rating and lower interest rate?

options.A: Pooling creates tradable securities that let the bank access more investors and free capital, lowering funding costs; tranching plus subordination and over‑collateralization protects senior payments from initial losses, raising their rating and lowering yields.
options.B: Pooling makes each loan safer so default risk falls for all tranches; therefore senior tranches get higher ratings and much lower yields.
options.C: Securitization removes loans from the bank's books so rating agencies automatically give senior tranches higher ratings regardless of structure.
options.D: Pooling converts loans into long‑term equity claims; senior tranches are higher rated simply because they receive all cash flows before others.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete \$100 million pool scenario to test three core ideas: securitization improving capital access/reducing funding cost, tranching allocating cash flows and losses, and credit enhancement via subordination/over‑collateralization raising senior ratings.
concepts_tested: Securitization pools assets to create tradable instruments and lower funding costs
Tranching allocates cash flows and losses to create different risk/return classes
Credit enhancement (subordination, over‑collateralization) raises senior tranche credit quality

questions.question: Two powder compacts of the same ceramic are sintered at the same temperature: sample A uses fine particles of radius $0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ and sample B uses coarse particles of radius $10\ \mu\text{m}$. Which sample will densify faster and why?

options.A: Sample A; smaller radius gives larger curvature ($\sim 1/r$), bigger chemical-potential gradients and faster diffusion that form necks and eliminate pores, lowering surface energy.
options.B: Sample B; larger particles have more mass so they flow into pores faster and densify more quickly.
options.C: Both densify at the same rate because sintering is controlled only by temperature, not particle size.
options.D: Neither densifies because sintering requires melting the material to form a solid mass.

questions.generation_notes: Created a comparative scenario (fine vs coarse particles) to test diffusion-driven neck growth, surface-energy as the thermodynamic driving force, and the role of particle curvature ($1/r$) and temperature on diffusion.
concepts_tested: Diffusion-driven neck formation and pore elimination
Thermodynamic driving force: surface area and surface energy reduction lowers free energy
Influence of particle size (curvature) and temperature on sintering/densification

questions.question: Reason-responsiveness says a belief is rational when the believer changes it in response to good reasons. Which scenario best shows why responding to reasons matters for forming rational beliefs?

options.A: Someone keeps believing a claim because changing it would disrupt their commitments, even though strong evidence contradicts it.
options.B: Someone revises a belief after getting strong, relevant new evidence, despite personal commitments or initial intuitions favoring the old belief.
options.C: Someone accepts a view because most people believe it, regardless of the actual evidence.
options.D: Someone fixes all their beliefs to save mental effort, even when there are strong reasons to reconsider them.

questions.simplification_notes: Question and options shortened and clarified; preserved the reason-responsiveness idea by using clear, concrete scenarios showing changing versus keeping beliefs.
concepts_tested: Reason-responsiveness as a criterion for rationality: rationality defined by responsiveness to reasons and how reasons justify actions or beliefs.

Coherence-based vs reason-responsive accounts: internal coherence among mental states versus responsiveness to external reasons, including how they handle contradictions and justify beliefs/intentions.

Types and limits of rationality (theoretical vs practical, ideal vs bounded, internalist vs externalist, normative questions): how different domains and constraints shape what counts as rational and when rationality may be limited or context-dependent.

questions.question: A country with three parties gets national votes $A=45\%$, $B=35\%$, $C=20\%$ and elects $100$ seats; under single-member winner-take-all districts the seat outcome is $60$–$30$–$10$, while a nationwide party-list PR system would give about $45$–$35$–$20$. Which statement best explains the difference and its connection to limits on voting systems?

options.A: Single-member winner-take-all districts award each seat to the district plurality, so national vote shares can produce disproportional seat totals; party-list PR uses quotas or divisors to match seats to vote share, and Arrow/Gibbard show no system can satisfy all fairness criteria or be completely strategy-proof.
options.B: Party-list proportional representation always prevents strategic voting and satisfies every fairness criterion, so it will always produce perfectly proportional and non-strategic outcomes.
options.C: Arrow's theorem proves any ranked voting method will always avoid the spoiler effect, so ranked ballots eliminate the need for tactical voting.
options.D: Winner-take-all in single-member districts decreases wasted votes and typically produces more proportional seat distributions than multi-member proportional systems.

questions.generation_notes: Set up a numerical scenario showing disproportional seats under single-member districts vs PR, then offered options tying seat allocation mechanics, district structure effects, and Arrow/Gibbard implications; only A correctly links all three.
concepts_tested: Seat allocation mechanisms and proportionality (quotas/divisors vs district plurality)
Effect of district structure (single-member winner-take-all vs nationwide multi-member PR) on representation
Fundamental limits from Arrow's and Gibbard's theorems (impossibility of perfect fairness and strategy-proofness)

questions.question: A town has 10 council seats; Party A gets 60% of votes and Party B gets 40%. Which option correctly states likely seats under proportional, winner-take-all and mixed systems and why a perfect voting system is impossible?

options.A: Proportional: about 6 seats A and 4 B; winner-take-all: A might win all 10; mixed: some seats by districts and some by party lists; perfect system impossible because systems need trade-offs and can be gamed.
options.B: Proportional: A gets all 10 seats; winner-take-all: always 6 A and 4 B; mixed: always exact proportional; a perfect system exists.
options.C: Proportional: 5 A and 5 B; winner-take-all: B always wins; mixed: same as winner-take-all; impossibility means only dictators work.
options.D: Proportional: A gets 9 and B 1; winner-take-all: results always proportional; mixed: never compensates; perfect system impossible because some people can’t vote.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete 10-seat example with 60% vs 40% votes to test proportional vs winner-take-all vs mixed outcomes and included the trade-off/strategic-voting idea from Arrow/Gibbard.
concepts_tested: How votes translate into seats and proportionality
Differences between single-winner/winner-take-all, proportional and mixed systems and their outcomes
Theoretical limits: trade-offs and strategic voting (Arrow's and Gibbard’s ideas)

questions.question: A student has 30 minutes to pick one of 50 smartphone models for school; they choose a phone that has enough battery life, camera quality, and fits their budget without checking every review. Which idea does this behavior best illustrate?

options.A: Satisficing under bounded rationality — choosing a 'good enough' option because of time and information limits.
options.B: Perfect optimization from rational-choice theory — the student found the utility-maximizing phone after full analysis.
options.C: Bounded selfishness — the student sacrificed their own benefit to help others.
options.D: Epsilon-optimization — the student deliberately ensured the phone's payoff is within $\epsilon$ of the true optimum.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete shopping scenario to contrast satisficing (bounded rationality) with optimization and related extensions like epsilon-optimization and bounded selfishness.
concepts_tested: bounded rationality
satisficing
difference between bounded rationality and rational-choice/optimization

questions.question: Voters elect a representative who votes to save a park even though most voters wanted a mall; a constitutional court later cancels the law. The legislature has two chambers and chooses the prime minister. Which option best explains why the representative acted differently, why the court canceled the law, and how this system shapes power?

options.A: He acted as a trustee using his judgment; the court checked the law against the constitution; a bicameral parliament that picks the prime minister splits power and adds checks.
options.B: He had absolute authority to do anything; the court only gives advice; two chambers make decisions faster.
options.C: He followed the direct will of voters exactly; the court canceled the law because most voters changed their mind; bicameral means people vote on every law.
options.D: He only followed his party bosses; the court canceled the law for money reasons; one chamber controls the prime minister.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short scenario linking trustee vs delegate behavior, judicial review as a constitutional check, and how bicameral parliamentary design divides power and adds checks.
concepts_tested: Delegated authority and trustee vs delegate tension
Checks and balances; judicial review and constitutional limits
Institutional design differences: bicameral and parliamentary effects on power and accountability

questions.question: A mayor shares residents' background but backs policies that hurt them. Voters can judge and replace the mayor. Which idea says he failed to "act for" residents, and what lets citizens punish him?

options.A: Substantive representation; voters can use elections to judge and remove the mayor.
options.B: Descriptive representation; sharing background proves he represents residents so voters cannot punish.
options.C: Promissory representation; voters punish by suing when promises are broken.
options.D: Gyroscopic representation; the mayor follows personal judgment and cannot be held accountable.

questions.generation_notes: Used a short, concrete scenario contrasting shared identity versus acting for interests; options test Pitkin's substantive view, accountability via elections, and Mansbridge-style alternatives as distractors.
concepts_tested: substantive vs descriptive representation
accountability through elections
alternative views of representative action (promissory, gyroscopic)

questions.question: Why does holding leaders responsible help them act for the people?

options.A: It proves leaders are like the people they represent.
options.B: It makes leaders show their private choices to voters.
options.C: It gives voters a way to judge leaders through elections that can remove them.
options.D: It forces leaders to follow majority wishes at the cost of small groups.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to keep the idea of citizens judging leaders.
concepts_tested: Substantive representation: representation as “acting for” the interests of the represented, not just descriptive likeness or symbolic signaling.
Accountability mechanism: citizens’ ability to judge and sanction representatives based on whether they act in the represented's best interests.

Theoretical frameworks for representation: Pitkin’s substantive view versus descriptive/symbolic views, and Mansbridge’s four democratic representations (promissory, anticipatory, surrogate, gyroscopic) as ways to explain how representatives act for people and how actions are normatively assessed.

questions.question: A town elects leaders, its courts protect a small group's school, and studies show democracies often have better health and schooling—what is the best conclusion?

options.A: Elections alone make a democracy and therefore always cause better health and education.
options.B: Elections plus legal protections matter, and better social outcomes often follow democracy but are not caused only by it.
options.C: Only direct votes by everyone count as democracy; courts are not needed.
options.D: Majorities should decide everything, even if minority rights or schools are harmed.

questions.generation_notes: Created a short scenario combining representative elections, constitutional/court protections, and study results; answer tests choosing balanced reasoning about institutions and causation.
concepts_tested: Direct vs representative democracy and institutional limits on majority power
Minimalist vs maximalist definitions of democracy (elections alone vs rights protections)
Correlation versus causation between democracy and social outcomes (health, education, economy)

questions.question: Why does democracy use rules with judges to protect small groups?

options.A: A written rulebook plus neutral judges protect small groups from unfair laws.
options.B: The majority can ignore small groups' protections.
options.C: Only judges make the laws in this case.
options.D: Courts stop elections by taking permanent power.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard phrases with simple words such as rules, judges, small groups.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Liberal democracy balances majority rule with minority rights through a constitutional framework and an independent judiciary.
Concept 2: Direct democracy vs. representative democracy as distinct mechanisms for citizen participation and power delegation.
Concept 3: The relationship between democracy and legitimacy/outcomes (e.g., health, education, economic outcomes) and the role of public opinion in sustaining democratic systems.

questions.question: A filmmaker adapts a copyrighted short story into a 90-minute movie, adds many new scenes and dialogue, and gives the film a distinct style. Which statement correctly describes the filmmaker's copyright rights in the movie and their relation to the original story?

options.A: The movie is a separate work; the filmmaker owns copyright only in the new, original material (the added scenes and style) and can control reproductions and distributions of the film, but cannot claim copyright over the original story's text without the original author's permission.
options.B: Because the film is based on the story, the filmmaker automatically owns full copyright over both the film and the original story, including the story's text and characters.
options.C: Any change, even small ones like new costumes or a few altered lines, makes the film fully original and grants the filmmaker exclusive rights to the whole story.
options.D: By making the film the filmmaker obtains the exclusive right to prepare all future derivative works from the original story, so others cannot adapt the story even if they create different new material.

questions.generation_notes: Created a concrete adaptation scenario to test independence of derivative works, originality/substantial transformation requirement, and the limited scope of derivative-work rights versus underlying work rights.
concepts_tested: Independence of derivative work and limitation of protection to new contributed material
Requirement of substantial transformation and originality for copyrightability

questions.question: A city starts free language classes, community sports teams, and mixed cultural events for newcomers; which statement best describes social integration and the most likely outcomes?

questions.question: How does chronic stress connect harmful social and economic conditions to worse health over time?

options.A: It mainly lowers access to medical care because people lose money and cannot pay for services.
options.B: It triggers neuroendocrine (e.g., cortisol) and inflammatory responses that cause 'wear-and-tear' on the body (allostatic load), raising disease risk over time.
options.C: It changes a person’s DNA sequence so they become genetically more likely to get disease.
options.D: It only causes short-term illness in childhood and has no long-term health effects.

Concept 2: Life-course perspective as a framework showing how conditions across life stages accumulate to affect health and functioning.
Concept 3: Chronic stress as a mediating mechanism by which adverse social/economic conditions lead to poorer health outcomes.

questions.question: How do social determinants of health affect people's health without medical care?

Concept 2: Life-course perspective: early-life social conditions influence health trajectories across the lifespan.
Concept 3: Policy and economic policy shape health equity by distributing resources and opportunities, producing health disparities.

questions.question: Why do different ways of showing the same risk — qualitative (low/medium/high), an absolute probability (e.g., $2\%$ per year), or a relative risk (e.g., $2\times$ higher) — change decisions?

options.A: Because qualitative labels give no basis for comparing situations.
options.B: Because each format highlights different things (magnitude, baseline risk, time horizon), changing how acceptable or urgent the risk seems and which mitigation is chosen.
options.C: Because absolute probabilities always make people choose more conservative actions than relative risks.
options.D: Because turning risk into numbers removes all uncertainty, so the format doesn't affect choices.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: The risk assessment framework and its sequence (hazard identification → likelihood/consequences estimation → risk evaluation → mitigation; hazard analysis as the first stage; risk management as the broader framework).

Concept 3: System-level risk and complexity (distinction between individual risk assessment and systems risk assessment, including linear vs. nonlinear systems and how complexity affects predictability).

questions.question: How does complex (nonlinear) system behavior affect predictions that assume simple cause and effect? What should risk models do when systems can change suddenly?

options.A: Linear models can capture all interactions if given enough parameters, so predictions stay reliable.
options.B: Complex systems have feedback, thresholds, and surprise behaviors that linear models miss. Risk models should allow for sudden regime shifts.
options.C: Nonlinear systems are inherently random, so complex models do not improve predictions. Simpler models are always preferable.
options.D: More data always makes nonlinear systems perfectly predictable, so complex models are unnecessary.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: The risk assessment process as a flow of steps (hazard analysis -> risk assessment -> risk evaluation) and how each step informs risk management decisions.
Concept 2: How risk results are interpreted and communicated, including cognitive biases and the role of decision aids in shaping decisions.
Concept 3: System complexity (linear vs nonlinear systems) and its impact on predictability and modeling in risk assessment.

questions.question: A town finds a chemical in its river and commissions an exposure assessment to guide cleanup. Which set of information from the exposure assessment best supports calculating risk and choosing remediation targets?

Exposure assessment quantifies magnitude, frequency, duration, routes/pathways, absorbed amount and bioavailability

questions.question: A researcher follows the same 500 people each month for 3 years to record their exercise levels and mood. Which statement best describes what this prospective longitudinal panel study can and cannot do?

options.A: Because it follows the same people prospectively, it reduces cohort effects and can show whether increases in exercise precede mood changes; however, as an observational study it cannot prove causation and may suffer attrition bias.
options.B: Tracking the same people means any differences are caused by exercise, so the study proves exercise causes mood change.
options.C: If the researcher used retrospective medical records instead, the study would avoid attrition and could prove causality more easily.
options.D: A cross-sectional snapshot of different people at one time would be equally good at observing within-person change over the 3-year period.

Temporal ordering in longitudinal data helps distinguish short- vs long-term change and informs causal interpretation but does not prove causation in observational designs
Differences between prospective and retrospective longitudinal designs and their biases (e.g., attrition, recall bias)

questions.question: Why do researchers use observational studies for harmful environmental exposures? How can these studies suggest the exposures caused the harm?

options.A: Because harmful exposures can be ethically assigned in trials, and observational studies are cheaper.
options.B: Because it would be unethical to put people into harmful exposures. They observe exposures and use criteria: temporality (cause before effect), dose–response (more exposure, worse effect), replication, and controlling confounding.
options.C: Because observational studies are free from confounding and can prove causation better than experiments.
options.D: Because environmental exposures do not vary between people and so cannot be tested in experiments.

questions.question: Why are fixed research designs more reliable when a researcher has a clear theory?

options.A: They set variables and hypotheses before data are collected, limiting researcher choices and reducing bias. This makes results more trustworthy.
options.B: Researchers collect data first and pick hypotheses later, increasing flexibility but raising bias.
options.C: Variables are found during data collection, helping exploration but making theory tests weaker.
options.D: They add new variables as the study goes, which is claimed to improve how well results apply elsewhere.

Fixed vs. flexible designs: theory-driven, pre-specified variables vs. adaptable inquiry when theory or quantitative measures are unavailable.
Epistemology/ontology shaping design: researchers' beliefs about knowledge and reality influence the choice of design, methods, and measures.

questions.question: Why does betrayal trauma cause PTSD-like symptoms that focus on anger and a broken trust, and how is this different from regular PTSD?

options.A: Because betrayal trauma is driven by fear of external threats, while regular PTSD is mainly anger-driven and caused by trusted sources.

options.C: Because betrayal trauma and regular PTSD are the same condition, with the only difference being how severe the event was.
options.D: Because betrayal trauma only happens in romantic relationships and never involves organizations or institutions.

questions.question: In Pontryagin's principle the costate $\lambda(t)$ and Hamiltonian $H(x,u,\lambda,t)=L(x,u,t)+\lambda^{T}f(x,u,t)$ are used. Why does minimizing $H$ with respect to $u$ at each time (keeping $x$ and $\lambda$ fixed) give a control law that is globally optimal under the principle's conditions?

options.A: Because $\lambda(t)$ is the shadow price of the state variables, and the term $\lambda^{T}f$ shows how current control choices change future costs through the dynamics; minimizing $H$ balances the immediate cost $L$ and future marginal cost, yielding a globally optimal trajectory when paired with the state and costate equations.
options.B: Because $H$ depends only on the current state and control, so future effects disappear and choosing $u$ to minimize $H$ at each moment must give the global optimum.
options.C: Because the costate $\lambda(t)$ is a fixed parameter chosen beforehand, so minimizing $H$ is a simple static optimization that trivially produces the optimal control for all times.
options.D: Because the dynamics $f(x,u,t)$ are assumed linear, $H$ becomes linear in $u$, and therefore instantaneous minimization always equals a globally optimal solution regardless of the cost structure.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one clear sentence, math written inline as LaTeX, options made concise and kept the original correct answer and core idea about the costate encoding future costs.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The optimization problem structure in optimal control — cost functional, state and control variables, dynamics, and constraints that define the objective and feasible solutions.

Concept 2: Core optimality principles — Pontryagin's maximum (minimum) principle as a necessary condition and the Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation as a sufficient condition, and what each implies for deriving the optimal control.

Concept 3: The control policy–system relationship — how a control law maps states to controls to steer the system toward minimizing the objective (e.g., minimizing travel time, fuel, or monetary cost), including trade-offs and constraints illustrated by examples.

questions.question: Why does the Bellman equation let us solve a finite-horizon problem by working backward? How does $V_i(y)$ relate to $V_{i+1}$?

options.A: Because the best decision at time $i$ depends only on the immediate reward and not on the future.
options.B: Because of optimal substructure: $V_i(y)=\max_a\{\text{immediate payoff}+V_{i+1}(\text{next state})\}$, so we can compute $V_{i+1}$ first and work back.
options.C: Because an optimal policy for the last step always makes all earlier steps optimal too.
options.D: Because $V_i(y)$ equals the sum of all future rewards regardless of actions, so backward steps are not needed.

questions.simplification_notes: Broke the question into two short sentences, used inline LaTeX for $V_i(y)$ and $V_{i+1}$, and kept options short and plausible.
concepts_tested: Optimal substructure and recursive decomposition
Bellman equation and backward induction (value functions Vi)
Recovery of the optimal decision sequence by tracking back through computations

questions.question: If the same nominal dose of a chemical is given by different routes (oral, inhalation, or skin), how does the route change the dose-response relationship?

options.A: It changes the fraction reaching systemic circulation and the target site because different routes have different absorption barriers and first-pass metabolism, so the effective dose and observed toxicity differ.
options.B: It does not affect toxicity; only the nominal dose matters because the chemical is the same.
options.C: It can change the chemical forms reaching the site because some routes cause metabolism that creates different active or toxic metabolites.
options.D: It only changes how fast effects start, not the size of the effect, because all routes eventually deliver the same total amount.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clear examples (oral, inhalation, skin), kept technical terms (absorption barriers, first-pass metabolism) but explained their role; options made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Dose-response principle: The toxicity of a substance depends on the dose and related exposure characteristics (e.g., duration, route).
Modulating factors in toxicity: Factors such as dosage, duration, route of exposure, species, age, sex, and environment influence the outcome.
The dose makes the poison (threshold/context dependence): Only the appropriate dose yields a toxic effect, highlighting that harm is a function of dose rather than the substance alone.

questions.question: Why do dimensionless groups formed from variables with different units reveal behavior that does not depend on the system's choice of units or its size?

options.A: Because they measure the relative sizes of competing effects and cancel units, turning governing equations into relations between pure numbers that show similarity and scaling.
options.B: Because they fix units to a single standard so every problem uses the same parameters.
options.C: Because they increase the number of independent variables, making models more flexible.
options.D: Because they turn physical laws into fitted empirical constants.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clear language ("dimensionless groups", "cancel units", "similarity and scaling"), and made all options concise and plausible while preserving the original correct choice.
concepts_tested: Dimensionless quantities are unit-independent ratios/parameters that enable comparison and scaling across different unit systems.
Dimensionless numbers (e.g., Reynolds number, fine-structure constant, Lorentz factor) serve as fundamental parameters that determine behavior or regime in physical, chemical, or geometric contexts.
Dimensional analysis and the Buckingham π theorem provide a principled method to derive dimensionless groups from variables, revealing relationships and reducing complexity in modeling.

questions.question: A student claims the drag force F (dimension $M L T^{-2}$) on a sphere depends on fluid density $\rho$ ($M L^{-3}$), speed $v$ ($L T^{-1}$), diameter $D$ ($L$) and viscosity $\mu$ ($M L^{-1} T^{-1}$); which statement is correct?

options.A: The formula $F = C\,\rho v^{2} D^{2}$ is dimensionally homogeneous and plausible; the relevant dimensionless group is $Re=\rho v D/\mu$.
options.B: The formula is inconsistent because you must multiply by $\mu$ to get $M L T^{-2}$; the correct dimensionless group is $F/(\rho v D)$.
options.C: This formula only works if you use SI units (metres, seconds); with feet and pounds it will fail.
options.D: No combination of $\rho,v,D,\mu$ can form a dimensionless group, so Buckingham\'s $\pi$ theorem cannot be used here.

questions.generation_notes: Used a concrete drag-force example to check dimensional homogeneity, emphasize units vs. dimensions, and ask for the relevant dimensionless Reynolds number (Buckingham π concept).
concepts_tested: dimensional homogeneity
dimension vs unit (unit-independence)
dimensionless groups / Buckingham π (Reynolds number)

Buckingham π theorem / nondimensionalization to form dimensionless groups

The accounting equation as a fundamental relationship: Assets = Liabilities + Equity.

Interconnections among financial statements: how net income affects equity and how the cash flow, income, and balance sheet statements relate through time (opening/closing balances, cash flows, and profit).

questions.question: A mid-size retailer moved from separate inventory, purchasing, and accounting apps to a cloud ERP-based AIS; accounts payable time fell from 48 hours to 3 hours, monthly reporting fell from 7 days to 6 hours, and annual software cost fell from \$120,000 to \$30,000. Which explanation best links ERP integration, automation, and cloud deployment to these outcomes?

options.A: ERP integration gives modules shared data so cross-functional processes (like automated three-way matching) run correctly and faster; automation improves timeliness and accuracy for managers, and cloud deployment lowers costs and scales while reducing routine transactional roles.
options.B: All improvements are due only to moving to the cloud because internet hosting speeds up processing and the ERP structure had no effect on data sharing or reports.
options.C: Automation made tasks faster but increased errors, and the cost drop to \$30,000 implies the company switched to low-quality software that harmed decision-making.
options.D: ERP modules duplicated data across systems so departments work independently; the time reductions are explained only by cutting staff and working longer hours.

questions.generation_notes: Created a concrete company scenario with before/after metrics and four concise options; the correct option ties ERP shared data, automation benefits, and cloud cost/scalability implications together.
concepts_tested: ERP integration and shared data enabling cross-functional processes
Automation improving timeliness and accuracy for decision-making
Cloud/packaged/ERP deployment effects on costs, scalability, and workforce roles
